section: R. R. Gossick, K. Henry Reactor Technology Division
text: The neutron energy spectrometer employs proton recoil pulses from a proportional counter, and has been described in ORNL-528.[8] During the past quarter, this device has been used to measure the energy spectrum of a Po-Be source and a mock fission source.

section: R. R. Gossick, K. Henry Reactor Technology Division
text: The curve indicated by a dotted line is the fission spectrum due to Bloch and Staub.(9) Assuming that the spectrum follows the dotted line, the total number of neutrons per second per curie is given by the area under the curve as $1.7 \times 10^{5}$ .

section: R. R. Gossick, K. Henry Reactor Technology Division
text: The integral of this curve gives a figure of $3.5 \times 10^6$ for the total number of neutrons per second per curie, which may be compared with Fermi's figure of $2.8 \times 10^6$ .

section: W. K. Ergen, F. H. Murray, S. Podgor, J. H. Trice, Reactor Technology Division
text: (The attenuations used by Bethe were higher by a factor of 10 which was due to his assumption of 30 ft reactor-crew-separation, whereas separation of 100 ft now appears feasible.) The weight of 67 long tons for a lead-water-shield around a 2 ft radius reactor was computed in this way, in satisfactory agreement with the weights computed from preliminary lid tank data.

Twenty sheets, $56\%$ in. $\times 66\%$ in. $\times 1/8$ in. of B₄C and Tygon (Tybor) has been fabricated for the Lid Tank Experiments.
B₄C and Tygon paint are mixed in the ratio of 3 parts B₄C to 1 part paint by hand.
This gives $60\%$ B₄C by volume.
Thickness can be varied from 1/16 in. to 1/8 in.
Tensile strength of "Tybor" is 215 psi and age has no apparent effect on the strength.
Elongation is about $11\%$ .
The material is not brittle at temperatures of $40 - 50^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Experimental 4 in. $\times$ 4 in. $\times$ 48 in. squares of tungsten carbide and Tygon have been made.

Ratio of $\mathsf{W}_2\mathsf{C}$ /Tygon is 18 to 1. $\mathsf{W}_2\mathsf{C}$ in the desired particle size range is not commercially available to permit making sheets $56\%$ in. $\times$ $66\%$ in. $\times$ 1/8 in., but limited personnel has postponed this operation.
Tensile strength of $\mathsf{W}_2\mathsf{C}$ -Tygon sheets will be determined to see whether or not a sheet $56\%$ in. $\times$ $66\%$ in. $\times$ 1/8 in. can support its own weight.
The sheet will weigh approximately 150 lbs as compared to 25 lbs for $\mathsf{B}_4\mathsf{C}$ -Tygon sheet of equivalent dimensions.
A density of $4.7 \, \text{gm/cc}$ was obtained with a compressive strength of 3200 psi.
Two samples of MO and MI (Boron containing) cements were irradiated in Hole 19 in the X-10 pile for approximately 1000 hours.
The equilibrium moisture content of MO cement was determined at $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ and $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
Air with a relative humidity of $17\%$ was passed over the cement which was in an oil bath at $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .
The cement was heated up to $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and again equilibrium was assumed when no loss in weight was noticed.
The temperature was then dropped to $200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and the sample was allowed to come to equilibrium, raised to $300^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , etc.
The test was continued for 37 days.
Boral, as described in ORNL-242,(15) is an engineering material for the absorption of thermal neutrons without production of hard gammas in a form which allows easy heat removal.
Two large ingots 27 in. × 36 in. × 6 in. were scheduled for rolling into sheets, 56½ in. × 66½ in. × 3/16 in.
The picture frame which confines the ingo has been increased from 1 in. to 5½ in. in thickness; the covers (cladding) have been increased from ½ in. to ½ in. thick.
A temperature of 1100°F at the center of the core material is desired before rolling.
An estimate of the cost of fabricating a Boral sheet (50-50) is about $15/ft².
It is also estimated that a sheet of boral, 1/8 in. thick will attenuate thermal neutrons by a factor of 10⁵.
Thermal conductivity specimens have been made by hot pressing $\mathbf{B}_{\phi}\mathbf{C}$ and Al powder in a graphite die at $1200^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ .

In the sorting tests to date, samples of the more readily available materials being considered have been exposed to liquid lithium in evacuated and welded Armco iron capsules in four-hour tests at $1830^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , and a similar series has been run using bismuth instead of lithium.
Figure 5 shows the design of the modified capsule being used in these tests and its relation to sample and liquid coolant.

Preliminary four-hour results from lithium tests at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ , which must be rigidly checked before they can be accepted as conclusive, indicate (l) iron, molydbenum, and zirconium suffered less weight change than the other materials tested, (2) tungsten, tantalum, and columbium were not severely attacked, and (3) cobalt and nickel underwent substantial weight loss.

The four-hour, $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ tests in bismuth, subject to the same or similar qualifications as the lithium tests, showed nickel and zirconium to be severely attacked, whereas visual observation did not indicate attack of the iron, tungsten, tantalum, molybdenum, and columbium.
The next series of tests is being conducted at $1800^{\circ}\mathrm{F}$ for forty hours, and will be followed by tests for still longer periods of time and tests at other temperatures.

In the case of sodium, removal of oxygen has been accomplished by calcium-getting at about $500^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , followed by filtration just above the melting point of sodium to remove excess oxides and calcium.
Calcium forms a eutectic with lithium at $2\%$ calcium and consequently cannot be completely removed from lithium by filtration.

These eight chambers are arranged in a circle and geared to an indexing handle so that a source may be received in any of the eight chambers and then rotated $180^{\circ}$ to a position from which it may be dropped into the source shield.

When the gold is to be reinserted in the pile the source shield is brought up under the receiving shield by means of a lift truck, the gold cylinder is then blown from the source shield into the receiving shield, and rotated $180^{\circ}$ to a position from which it is blown into the pile.

It seems likely that high purity $^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ would be very valuable as either a coolant or a fuel-element constituent for an aircraft reactor.
This method appears highly suitable for quantity production of $^7\mathrm{Li}$ if it develops satisfactorily.
The molecular distillation (non-equilibrium) of lithium metal should result in a concentration of $^6$ Li in the vapor and of $^7$ Li in the residue.

The ratio of the rates of diffusion of $^6$ Li and $^7$ Li from the liquid metal is proportional to the inverse square root of the masses, and a theoretical separation factor ( $\alpha$ ) is calculated to be 1.08 for this process.
Preliminary experiments with a single stage molecular still have shown a separation factor = 1.018.
In a typical experiment, 10.1 grams of lithium were distilled over a period of about two hours at $450^{\circ}$ C and 15 microns pressure until only 1 gram remained in the still pot.
The residual material in the still pot assayed $92.935 \pm 0.015\%$ $^7$ Li as compared with a starting material assay of $92.500 \pm 0.020\%$ .
In the latest experiment the column was charged with LiOH, and over 170 hours were required for the lithium to wash through a 24 inch column.
In an experiment at X-10 with a "fixed" bed of resin, the concentrations of $^7\mathrm{Li}$ in the leading and trailing edges were reported to be $93.4\%$ and $90.0\%$ , respectively.

By maintaining a cold column at the top and a hot column at the bottom, the $^7\mathrm{Li}$ isotope is concentrated in the bottom of the hot column and at the top of the cold while $^6\mathrm{Li}$ is concentrated at the top of the hot column and in the bottom of the cold column.
If these can be overcome successfully, the production of $^7\mathrm{Li}$ will become a reality.
Single stage separation factors as high as 1.05, 1.06, and 1.07 have been reported for experiments in which lithium is reduced at a changing mercury cathode in an aqueous lithium solution.
As the lithium amalgam is continuously removed, the concentration of ${}^{7}\mathrm{Li}$ remaining in solution is increased.
